headline: The 2024 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball Draft: Where Champions Were Built and Dreams Went to Die
subheadline: Ronald Acuña Jr. went first overall, Chris Sale went 133rd, and somewhere in between, the Buttville Skidmarks quietly assembled a roster that would haunt everyone's nightmares.
body:
**I. The War Room Opens**

There's a scene in *Moneyball* where Billy Beane is on the phone during the trade deadline, working three deals simultaneously, eating sunflower seeds like a man possessed, and you think to yourself: this is what sports obsession looks like at its purest form. That scene has nothing—and I mean *nothing*—on fourteen grown adults logging into a fantasy baseball draft room on March 22nd, 2024, armed with spreadsheets, projection systems, and the kind of caffeine intake that would concern a cardiologist. This is MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball, where the snake draft format separates the visionaries from the victims, and where one bad first-round pick can haunt you like that time you texted your ex at 2 AM.

The 2024 draft had everything: a consensus 1-1 pick in **Ronald Acuña Jr.** that felt as safe as LeBron going to Cleveland (spoiler alert: it wasn't), a defending champion in **BaseballPro77** trying to repeat, and a team literally called the **Buttville Skidmarks** that would somehow emerge as the most prescient drafters in the room. We had reaches that made you physically uncomfortable. We had steals that looked like typos. We had **North Correa** drafting **Mike Trout** in the 4th round like it was 2019 and injuries were a myth. And through it all, we had the unmistakable feeling that someone was going to look very, very smart—and someone else was going to spend six months wondering where it all went wrong.

The biggest tease? **Chris Sale** going 133rd overall to the **Albuquerque Bombers**, who already had a championship pedigree and apparently also had a time machine. **Tarik Skubal** sliding to pick 42, which in retrospect feels like finding a Picasso at a garage sale. And **Jarren Duran**—yes, *that* Jarren Duran—going with the 204th pick, dead last in round 15, to a team that was already stacked. But we'll get there. Oh, we'll get there.

**II. Draft Order & Early-Pick Breakdown**

The draft order gods smiled upon **North Correa** with the first overall selection, and they did what anyone with functioning brain cells would do: they took **Ronald Acuña Jr.**, the reigning NL MVP, a 30-30-30 threat, the kind of player you build a fantasy roster around. It was the obvious pick. It was the *right* pick. It was also the pick that would blow up in their face faster than a Fyre Festival investment when Acuña tore his ACL in May. (We'll discuss the cosmic cruelty of this later, but just know that having the first overall pick and ending up with 36 games of production is the fantasy baseball equivalent of ordering a steak and receiving a photo of a cow.)

After that, the top of the draft went mostly chalk: **Julio Rodríguez** to the **Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs** at 2 (solid but unspectacular), **Corbin Burnes** to the **Benchwarmers** at 3 (the rare SP1 selection that actually paid off), **Mookie Betts** to the **Central Wisconsin Curd Fryers** at 4 (elite until his hand wasn't), and then **Spencer Strider** to the **Riverside Roughnecks** at 5. And here's where things got spicy: Strider, who was supposed to anchor rotations for a decade, threw 24.2 innings before his UCL waved the white flag. The Roughnecks essentially burned their first-round pick on a guy who contributed less than some waiver wire streamers. That's not bad luck—that's the universe teaching you a lesson about pitcher fragility.

**III. The Draft Grades: All 14 Teams**

**North Correa (Draft Pick #1)**
Let's start with the elephant in the room: **Ronald Acuña Jr.** went down in May, turning a franchise cornerstone into a $0 FAAB claim's worth of production. But the carnage didn't stop there. **Mike Trout** in Round 4? He played 29 games. **Blake Snell** in Round 5? A 3.12 ERA sounds nice until you realize he threw only 104 innings with a 1.05 WHIP that was weirdly pedestrian. **Shane Bieber** in Round 6? Tommy John surgery. **Yu Darvish** in Round 9? Fourteen starts, 4.08 ERA. This roster reads like a fantasy baseball injury ward. The bright spot? **Reynaldo López** in Round 8 actually delivered a 1.83 ERA and 0.94 WHIP in 93.2 IP as a reliever, which is like finding a twenty-dollar bill in jeans you're about to donate. But you can't build a season on one middle-relief jackpot. **Grade: D+** (The draft equivalent of buying a lottery ticket and then immediately setting it on fire.)

**Wonder Wharf Wonderdogs (Draft Pick #2)**
A team that finished 2nd in 2022 and 4th in 2023, the Wonderdogs came in with pedigree and drafted like it. **Julio Rodríguez** at 1.02 was fine—21 HR, 82 RBI, 25 SB is solid if unspectacular production—but the real story here is **Gunnar Henderson** in Round 3. Henderson posted a .911 OPS with 37 HR, 87 RBI, and 5.8 fWAR as a 23-year-old. That's a top-15 overall player falling to pick 30. **Shota Imanaga** at pick 139 (Round 10!) delivered a 2.91 ERA and 1.08 WHIP in 173.1 IP as a rookie. That's borderline larceny. The concerns? **Freddy Peralta** in Round 4 was solid but not elite (3.68 ERA), and **Esteury Ruiz** in Round 8 was basically replacement level. But when your Round 3 and Round 10 picks both outperform their draft position by a mile, you did something right. **Grade: B+** (The Wonderdogs drafted like they'd seen this movie before.)

**Benchwarmers (Draft Pick #3)**
After finishing 14th in 2023—dead last, for those keeping score—the Benchwarmers needed a miracle. They didn't get one, but they got **Corbin Burnes** at 1.03, who delivered a 2.92 ERA and 181 K in 194.1 IP. That's your SP1 doing SP1 things. **Zack Wheeler** in Round 3 was even better: 2.57 ERA, 1.04 WHIP, 200 K. The problem? **Marcus Semien** in Round 2 posted an .803 OPS with 24 HR, which is fine, but **Jackson Chourio** in Round 8 was the sneaky value play—.239 AVG but 21 HR and 22 SB from a 20-year-old. **Cody Bellinger** in Round 4 gave them a solid .842 OPS. This is a roster that should have competed, and if the Benchwarmers didn't climb out of the basement, it wasn't for lack of draft-day effort. **Grade: B** (From last place to respectable in one offseason—that's called progress.)

**Central Wisconsin Curd Fryers (Draft Pick #4)**
The Curd Fryers made the kind of draft-day decisions that look brilliant until reality intervenes. **Mookie Betts** at 1.04 was elite—.984 OPS, 1.0 fWAR—until a broken hand ended his season in June. **Tyler Glasnow** in Round 4 was dominant when healthy (3.49 ERA, 168 K in 136 IP) but that's the key phrase: *when healthy*. **Gerrit Cole** in Round 10 was the steal of the draft for anyone brave enough to wait on him—3.41 ERA, 99 K in 95 IP after returning from injury. **Anthony Santander** in Round 11 absolutely mashed: .814 OPS, 44 HR, 102 RBI. That's Round 11! The Curd Fryers drafted like they had inside information and then watched their first-rounder break. **Grade: B+** (Elite process, unfortunate results at the top.)

**Riverside Roughnecks (Draft Pick #5)**
Oh, the Roughnecks. Oh, no. **Spencer Strider** at 1.05 was a perfectly defensible pick—elite K upside, track record of dominance—and then his elbow exploded. He threw 24.2 innings. Twenty-four. That's not a first-round pick; that's a cautionary tale. **Vlad Guerrero Jr.** in Round 2 bounced back with a .940 OPS and 30 HR, which was legitimately excellent. **Elly De La Cruz** in Round 3 delivered 25 HR and 67 SB (SIXTY-SEVEN!), making him one of the most valuable fantasy assets in baseball. **Ketel Marte** in Round 7 posted an .878 OPS. There's a lot to like here... if you ignore that their first-round pick contributed less than some guys who got DFA'd in April. **Grade: C+** (The De La Cruz pick saved this from being a disaster.)

**Athletic Park Dragons (Draft Pick #6)**
**Bobby Witt Jr.** at 1.06 is the pick everyone wishes they had. Witt posted a .332 AVG, .927 OPS, 32 HR, 94 RBI, and 31 SB. That's a top-3 overall fantasy season. The Dragons followed it up with **Pete Alonso** in Round 2 (34 HR, 88 RBI), **Jose Altuve** in Round 3 (.778 OPS, solid but not vintage), and **Jazz Chisholm Jr.** in Round 4, who delivered an .830 OPS with 24 HR and 40 SB after moving to the Yankees. **Ryan Helsley** in Round 10 was elite (1.21 ERA, 49 saves). This is a draft that looks like it was conducted by someone who actually knows what they're doing. **Grade: A-** (Witt carried them, but the supporting cast showed up too.)

**Abbotsford-Colby Twins (Draft Pick #7)**
The Twins have finished 4th (2022) and 8th (2023), so they're trending in the wrong direction, and this draft didn't help. **Corbin Carroll** at 1.07 was a sophomore slump special: .689 OPS, 16 HR, 24 SB. That's not terrible, but it's a massive regression from his rookie campaign. **Adolis García** in Round 2 was solid (.762 OPS, 30 HR), but **Jackson Merrill** in Round 15 was the real find: .292 AVG, .818 OPS, 24 HR as a rookie. **Nathan Eovaldi** in Round 10 delivered a 3.80 ERA in 170.2 IP, which is useful if unsexy. The problem is the middle rounds didn't pop, and when your first-rounder underperforms, you need those mid-round hits. **Grade: C** (Carroll's regression sank this class.)

**Albuquerque Bombers (Draft Pick #8)**
The defending league participants (champions in 2022, top-5 finishes every year since 2021) drafted like they had something to prove. **Freddie Freeman** at 1.08 delivered an .854 OPS with 22 HR and 89 RBI—solid, consistent, exactly what you want from a first-rounder. **Austin Riley** in Round 2 was elite: .895 OPS, 33 HR, 97 RBI. But here's where it gets fun: **Chris Sale** at pick 133 (Round 10!) posted a 2.38 ERA, 0.92 WHIP, and 225 K in 177.2 IP. That's a Cy Young-caliber season in Round 10. **Jarren Duran** at pick 204 (Round 15!) hit .285 with 21 HR, 75 RBI, and 36 SB. The Bombers didn't just draft well; they drafted like they had access to information from the future. **Grade: A** (This is how you build a dynasty.)

**Sugar Land Skeeters (Draft Pick #9)**
Back-to-back bottom-three finishes (12th in 2022, 13th in 2023) suggest the Skeeters need to re-evaluate their approach. **Juan Soto** at 1.09 was excellent (.989 OPS, 41 HR, 109 RBI), and **Bryce Harper** in Round 2 crushed (.971 OPS, 30 HR). That's an elite 1-2 punch. **Kyle Schwarber** in Round 5 added 38 HR. The pitching is where things get dicey: **Luis Castillo** in Round 3 posted a 3.40 ERA, which is fine, but **Kevin Gausman** in Round 4 was just okay (4.30 ERA). **Francisco Alvarez** in Round 12 struggled behind the plate. When you're picking 9th overall and you get *this* much production from your first two picks, you should be competing. If the Skeeters didn't improve, something is structurally wrong. **Grade: B** (Top-heavy roster that needed more depth.)

**General Hospital (Draft Pick #10)**
**Kyle Tucker** at 1.10 was a home run—literally (.906 OPS, 23 HR in 78 games) until he got injured. **Ozzie Albies** in Round 2 played only 84 games. **Josh Jung** in Round 8? Wrist surgery, 18 games. The team name is "General Hospital," and they drafted like they were trying to fill it. **Bobby Miller** in Round 6 had a 7.04 ERA. **Carlos Rodón** in Round 13 had a 4.11 ERA. There's talent on this roster when healthy, but fantasy baseball rewards availability, and this team had none of it. **Grade: D** (The name became a prophecy.)

**BaseballPro77 (Draft Pick #11)**
The defending champion (2023 title, consistent top-5 finishes) drafted with the confidence of a team that knows what it's doing. **Aaron Judge** at 1.11 delivered an MVP-caliber season: .633 SLG, 58 HR, 144 RBI. That's the best hitter in baseball, and they got him at pick 11. **José Ramírez** in Round 2 was his usual elite self (.911 OPS, 39 HR, 118 RBI, 20 SB). **Bo Bichette** in Round 3 was a disappointment (.674 OPS in 81 games), but **Sonny Gray** in Round 9 delivered a 2.79 ERA and 3.07 FIP. **Riley Greene** in Round 12 posted an .817 OPS with 24 HR. When you have Judge and JRam anchoring your roster, you can survive some misses. **Grade: A-** (Champions draft like champions.)

**Hiroshima Radiation (Draft Pick #12)**
Consecutive top-3 finishes (3rd in 2022, 2nd in 2023) had the Radiation poised for a title run, and they drafted accordingly. **Fernando Tatis Jr.** at 1.12 bounced back with an .843 OPS, 21 HR, and 20 SB in 122 games. **Pablo López** in Round 2 delivered a 3.76 ERA and 195 K. **George Kirby** in Round 3 was excellent (3.35 ERA, 1.07 WHIP). **Willy Adames** in Round 14 mashed (.867 OPS, 32 HR, 112 RBI). The pitching depth is enviable, and Tatis staying relatively healthy was a pleasant surprise. **Grade: B+** (A contender's draft from a contender's franchise.)

**Canberra Wombats (Draft Pick #13)**
6th in 2022, 3rd in 2023—the Wombats are the picture of consistency, and this draft reflects their approach. **Matt Olson** at 1.13 rebounded to a .760 OPS with 28 HR, which is fine if unspectacular. **Trea Turner** in Round 2 posted an .837 OPS with 21 HR and 20 SB. **Yoshinobu Yamamoto** in Round 4 had a 3.00 ERA but only 90 IP due to injury. **Brandon Pfaadt** in Round 15 was a sneaky value play (4.30 ERA, 181 K in 184.1 IP). This is a safe, boring, competent draft. Nothing exciting, nothing disastrous. **Grade: B-** (The Toyota Camry of draft classes.)

**Buttville Skidmarks (Draft Pick #14)**
Picking last is supposed to be a disadvantage. The Skidmarks treated it like a cheat code. **Shohei Ohtani (Batter)** at 1.14 delivered an MVP season: .310/.390/.646, 54 HR, 130 RBI, 59 SB. That's not a typo. Fifty-four home runs. Fifty-nine stolen bases. From pick 14. **Yordan Alvarez** in Round 2 posted a .901 OPS with 35 HR. **Tarik Skubal** in Round 3—Round 3!—won the Cy Young with a 2.39 ERA, 0.92 WHIP, and 228 K. **Cole Ragans** in Round 6 delivered a 3.31 ERA and 223 K. **Michael King** in Round 9 had a 2.95 ERA. This draft class is disgusting in the best possible way. They got the league MVP at pick 14 and the Cy Young winner at pick 42. **Grade: A+** (This is the greatest draft class in league history. I'm not exaggerating.)

**IV. Draft Day Heroes & Villains**

**The 3 Best Value Picks:**

1. **Round 3, Pick 42: Tarik Skubal (Buttville Skidmarks)** — A Cy Young winner with a 2.39 ERA and 228 K falling to the third round is criminal. Every team that passed on him should be investigated for fantasy malpractice.

2. **Round 10, Pick 133: Chris Sale (Albuquerque Bombers)** — A 2.38 ERA, 0.92 WHIP, and 225 K in Round 10. Sale was written off as an injury risk, and the Bombers got one of the best pitcher seasons of the decade for the price of a dart throw.

3. **Round 15, Pick 204: Jarren Duran (Albuquerque Bombers)** — The last pick of Round 15 turned into a .285/21/75/36 season. The Bombers got an All-Star caliber outfielder with what was essentially a free roster spot.

**The 3 Worst Reaches/Decisions:**

1. **Round 1, Pick 5: Spencer Strider (Riverside Roughnecks)** — Yes, Strider was elite. Yes, elbow injuries happen. But spending a top-5 pick on a pitcher coming off astronomical workload concerns was always risky. He threw 24.2 innings. Twenty-four.

2. **Round 4, Pick 56: Mike Trout (North Correa)** — Trout in Round 4 feels like buying a classic car that's been in three accidents. You want to believe, but the evidence suggests otherwise. He played 29 games. Hope is not a strategy.

3. **Round 6, Pick 75: Bobby Miller (General Hospital)** — A 7.04 ERA from your Round 6 pitcher is catastrophic. Miller had the stuff, but the results were ugly, and there were safer options available at this slot.

**V. The Verdict: How the Draft Shaped the 2024 Season**

Looking back at the 2024 season, it's remarkable how clearly the draft winners and losers separated themselves. The **Buttville Skidmarks**, drafting from the supposedly disadvantageous 14th slot, assembled a roster that featured the league MVP (**Shohei Ohtani**, 54 HR, 59 SB, .646 SLG) and the Cy Young winner (**Tarik Skubal**, 2.39 ERA, 228 K) on the same team. That's not drafting well—that's drafting *historically*. If they didn't win this league, the scoring system is broken. They had the two best fantasy assets in baseball, both acquired before pick 43.

The **Albuquerque Bombers** proved that championship-caliber franchises don't rebuild—they reload. With **Chris Sale** delivering a Cy Young-caliber season from Round 10 and **Jarren Duran** emerging as a legitimate fantasy star from Round 15, they supplemented their solid early-round foundation (**Freddie Freeman**, **Austin Riley**) with late-round gems that would make Billy Beane weep. Their draft class had the highest floor *and* the highest ceiling, which is almost impossible to achieve.

On the other end of the spectrum, **North Correa** experienced the fantasy equivalent of getting struck by lightning while holding a winning lottery ticket. **Ronald Acuña Jr.**, **Mike Trout**, and **Shane Bieber** all suffered season-ending or season-limiting injuries. Their draft wasn't poorly conceived—it was cursed. Meanwhile, **General Hospital** lived up to their name in the worst way possible, with **Kyle Tucker**, **Ozzie Albies**, and **Josh Jung** all missing significant time. Sometimes the injury bug finds you; sometimes you draft directly into its path.

The **Athletic Park Dragons** were the quiet winners of the draft, securing **Bobby Witt Jr.** at pick 6 and watching him deliver one of the best fantasy seasons in recent memory (.927 OPS, 32 HR, 31 SB). Their balanced approach—elite first-rounder, solid supporting cast, reliable closer in **Ryan Helsley**—is the template other teams should study. They didn't swing for the fences on every pick; they just made consistently smart decisions.

**VI. The 2024 Draft: Final Grades & Legacy**

**Overall Draft Class Grade: B+**

This was a draft defined by extremes. The top end—**Ohtani**, **Skubal**, **Sale**, **Witt**, **Judge**—delivered historic seasons. The bottom end—**Strider**, **Acuña**, **Trout**—reminded everyone why pitcher injuries and age-related decline are the twin horsemen of fantasy baseball apocalypse. The middle was a mixed bag of solid production and missed opportunities.

In retrospect, the 2024 MillerLite® BeerLeagueBaseball Draft will be remembered for one thing above all else: the **Buttville Skidmarks** proving that draft position matters far less than draft intelligence. They got the MVP and the Cy Young from the 14th slot. That's not luck—that's vision. Meanwhile, **North Correa** learned the cruelest lesson fantasy baseball can teach: even the perfect pick can become worthless in an instant.

The managers who trusted their process—**Albuquerque's** late-round aggression, **Athletic Park's** balanced approach, **Buttville's** willingness to bet on elite talent regardless of slot—were rewarded. The managers who chased names over sustainability paid the price. And somewhere, **Chris Sale** is throwing a bullpen session, wondering why everyone gave up on him before he reminded them exactly who he is.

This draft had everything: heartbreak, vindication, and a team called the Skidmarks winning the whole damn thing. Fantasy baseball, baby. You can't make this stuff up.
